_patch_meta.session_ref: M08 Phase 2 Pass A meaning record via Claude API
_patch_meta.source: Claude API meaning record (Sonnet 4.6); 445 verses across 47 terms

set.analysis_note: Joseph's domineering position is expressed through sovereign administrative control over the land, such that all people—including his brothers—must bow before him, showing his authority operates through structural power over others.

The Israelites go out 'with a high hand'—defiantly and boldly—indicating an inner posture of raised self-assertion, here directed against Pharaoh's pursuit, enabled by God's prior act of hardening Pharaoh's heart.
God's 'rising up' is not human arrogance but triumphant divine majesty: he has gloriously surpassed the enemy by casting horse and rider into the sea, evoking exaltation through his victorious act.
The will of the worshipper is directed upward toward God in exaltation—'I will exalt him'—making the inner act of lifting up God the response to personal salvation and identity as 'my God.'

God's majesty (ga.on) here is the inner greatness that drives destructive power against adversaries—it is the source from which fury flows outward, consuming like fire, showing pride as divine sovereign force, not human vanity.
Miriam's refrain echoes Moses': God has 'risen up' gloriously in triumph over horse and rider, framing his rising as victorious exaltation expressed through the act of defeating Egypt at the sea.

Israel's 'pride of power' is the inner confidence that sustains their strength; God threatens to break it, showing it resides in the will as self-reliant assurance, whose collapse renders the nation vulnerable.

Acting 'with a high hand' describes an inner posture of defiant self-exaltation against God, where the will deliberately overrides divine command—here treated as equivalent to reviling the Lord directly.

Pride is located explicitly in the heart: prosperity can cause the heart to 'be lifted up,' producing forgetfulness of God—showing that self-exaltation is a heart-level movement that crowds out gratitude and memory.
Arrogance here is the inner presumption that drives a man to disobey appointed religious and legal authority; it is an act of will that defies God's established order and is treated as capital evil.
The king's heart must not be 'lifted up above his brothers'—pride is located in the heart as a hierarchical self-elevation over peers, which, if unchecked, leads to deviation from God's commandments.

A false prophet's presumption is an arrogant inner claim to divine authority—speaking a word God did not give—showing arrogance as the will usurping God's voice, exposed when the word fails to come true.

The enemy's potential self-exaltation ('our hand is triumphant') is a prideful misattribution of victory to themselves rather than God—an inner error of perception and boasting that God's restraint prevents from being voiced.
God's majesty (ga.a.vah) is expressed through his riding through the heavens to help Israel—here it is divine grandeur in action, a glory that moves purposefully on behalf of his people.
Israel's 'triumph' (ga.a.vah) is God-granted and expressed as the sword that defeats enemies—the pride here belongs to God's saving act, giving Israel a dignity that causes enemies to submit.

Hannah's horn being 'exalted in the Lord' locates the lifting up in the will and emotion—her heart exults and her horn rises not through self-achievement but through God's act, making exaltation an outcome of divine salvation.

Proud talk is the outward expression of an inner arrogance that flows from the mouth; God's omniscient weighing of actions stands as the structural check against it, showing that such pride misjudges its own standing before God.
Exaltation belongs entirely to God's sovereign will—he raises and lowers as he chooses—removing self-exaltation from human agency and placing it wholly in divine prerogative.
God's lifting of the needy to seats of honor shows exaltation as a divine reversal of social and inner status—the lowly are raised by God's action, not by their own will or merit.
God exalts the horn of his anointed king—the lifting up of the king's dignity and power is God's gift conferred through judgment and strength, not the king's own self-elevation.
Man judges by outward height of stature, but God looks at the heart—showing that the 'high' quality man prizes in appearance is not what God values; inward character (the heart) is the true measure.

Physical height (qo.mah) is what the human eye prizes in a potential king, but God explicitly overrides this outward measure by looking at the heart, exposing the inner person as the real locus of worth.

Eliab accuses David of 'presumption' and 'evil of heart' together—locating arrogance in the heart as an inner disposition that drives reckless, self-aggrandizing action, here the desire to witness battle.

God sends 'from on high' to rescue—the height here is God's elevated position as deliverer, and the verse evidences no human pride; rather it shows divine agency acting downward from a position of supreme height.

God's eyes are on the haughty to bring them down—pride here is the inner posture that draws divine opposition, while humility draws salvation, showing that God actively counters the self-elevated will.
David calls God 'exalted'—the lifting up is directed entirely toward God as an act of worship and recognition of his supreme status, expressing the inner will's orientation of praise upward.
God exalted David above his enemies—the elevation is experienced as deliverance from violence, showing exaltation as a God-given outcome that raises the rescued person above those who threatened him.
Jeroboam 'lifted up his hand' against the king—the inner will asserts itself in rebellion against royal authority, showing self-exaltation expressed through an act of defiant opposition.

Jeroboam's raising of his hand against Solomon is traced to specific circumstances, showing that the inner impulse to self-exalt against authority can be provoked by grievances about perceived injustice or ambition.
God exalted Jeroboam from among the people—the lifting up was entirely God's initiative and gift, making Jeroboam's subsequent sin an ingratitude against the very one who raised him.

God raised Omri 'out of the dust' to leadership—the exaltation is portrayed as divine gift from a position of nothing, which makes his subsequent sin of leading Israel into corruption a betrayal of that elevation.

The proverb warns against boasting before battle is won—the inner act of premature self-glorification is foolish because it claims a victory not yet secured, contrasting the one arming with the one who has already triumphed.

The Assyrian king has lifted his eyes to the heights in arrogant mockery directed against the Holy One of Israel—the height becomes the object of defiant self-elevation, which is framed as direct offense against God.
The Assyrian's raised voice against God is an act of inner defiance—self-exaltation of the will directed against the Holy One—making pride here an act of spiritual aggression against the divine.

The Assyrian king boasts of ascending 'the heights of the mountains'—his self-elevation is expressed through conquest of towering physical heights, becoming a metaphor for the inner pride that sets itself above all limits.

The 'tallest cedars' (qo.mah) are felled by the boasting king—physical height is used as a measure of what his pride has conquered, showing that self-elevation expresses itself by bringing down what is great and lofty.
Boasting in God's holy name directs the inner faculty of glorying outward toward God rather than the self—it is exultation whose object is divine identity, producing joy in those who seek the Lord.
God's promise to exalt Heman is fulfilled through the gift of many children—exaltation is here a concrete divine blessing that elevates the person's standing and legacy, not a human self-assertion.

Hezekiah humbled himself specifically 'for the pride of his heart'—pride is explicitly located in the heart as an inner disposition that, left unchecked, would have drawn God's wrath; humbling reverses that inward movement.

Ezra cannot lift his face to God because iniquity has 'risen higher than our heads'—the shame of guilt functions as an inward collapse that prevents the inner act of self-presentation before God, as pride is replaced by crushing guilt.

God's name is 'exalted above all blessing and praise'—the lifting up here is the worshipping community's recognition that God's majesty surpasses every human attempt to honor him, directing the inner act of praise upward.
God sets the lowly 'on high'—exaltation here is a reversal act of divine compassion that lifts the mourning and humble to safety, showing height as God's gift to those who lack self-elevation.

Job warns that if his head were 'lifted up' in pride, God would hunt him like a lion—inner self-exaltation would trigger divine opposition, showing that any rise of personal pride invites crushing pursuit.

God's wisdom is described as 'higher than heaven'—the height here speaks to divine transcendence that exceeds all human comprehension, with no human pride in view; the verse evidences only the inaccessible elevation of God's nature.

Job's witness and advocate are located 'on high' in heaven—the height is God's elevated position as righteous judge who sees Job's cause, offering no human boasting but comfort in divine witness above.

God's height in the heavens, above even the highest stars, establishes his transcendent elevation over creation—this is divine majesty at cosmic scale, not human pride, but it frames all human self-elevation as absurd by comparison.

Pride (ge.vah) is identified as the inner cause of a person's humiliation—when someone is brought low, it is attributed to pride within them, while God saves those who are lowly, showing pride as the inner disposition that draws downfall.

God makes peace in his 'high heaven'—the height is the domain of divine dominion and fearsome authority, with no human inner-being content in view; the verse simply locates God's sovereign peace-making in his lofty realm.

Job asks what his 'portion from the Almighty on high' would be—God's elevation is the source from which judgment and heritage flow down to humanity, framing height as the position of divine authority over human destiny.

God disciplines a person to 'conceal pride from a man'—pride is an inner disposition that God acts to suppress through correction, showing it as something embedded within the person that must be hidden away to redirect their conduct.
Pride in evil men is the inner disposition that causes God to withhold answers to their cries; it is a hardness of heart that blocks their access to divine response.
Here ga.on describes God's majestic, thunderous voice — a grandeur and sovereign power that is not pride in the negative sense but divine magnificence expressed in creation.
God challenges Job to clothe himself with majesty and dignity, using ga.on to denote a sovereign splendor that belongs properly to God alone, implicitly exposing Job's limits.
Go.vah here refers to divine dignity — the elevated grandeur God challenges Job to assume, implicitly contrasting human incapacity with God's genuine loftiness of being.
The proud person is one who is elevated in their own estimation, and God's challenge is to look on each such person and abase them, showing pride as an inner posture that invites divine humbling.
Pride here is an inner elevation that places the wicked alongside the proud, both deserving to be brought low; the proud person's standing is explicitly undone by divine action.
Leviathan's ga.vo.ah is his capacity to survey and dominate everything that is lofty; his inner orientation is one of supreme, unrivaled self-assertion over all proud creatures.
Leviathan reigns as king over 'sons of pride' — creatures whose defining inner trait is fierce, fearless self-assertion; sha.chats here denotes a daunting, haughty boldness of spirit.
God exalts the psalmist's head — an act directed at the will and dignity of the inner person, lifting a downcast soul to restored standing against those who sought to shame it.
Ma.rom here describes God returning to His high throne to preside over assembled peoples — height as the seat of divine authority and judgment, not human pride.
God's lifting the psalmist from the gates of death is an act of exaltation directed at the afflicted self — raising the endangered inner person from the lowest point to life.
Arrogance is the inner driving force behind the wicked person's hot pursuit of the poor — it is pride operating as agency, propelling oppressive action and entrapment of the vulnerable.
The wicked person boasts of what his own soul desires, making the self and its cravings the object of proud speech — an inner orientation that renounces God in favor of self-congratulation.
The wicked person's go.vah is a haughty face-posture that shuts out any seeking of God; pride here operates in the thought-life, producing atheistic self-sufficiency — 'There is no God.'
God's judgments are on high — at a height that the arrogant wicked cannot perceive or reach, so his pride blinds him to the divine standard that will ultimately judge him.
Vileness being exalted among humanity is the social/moral elevation of what is base — rum here describes a perverse upward movement where the wrong inner qualities gain public honor.
The enemy's exaltation over the psalmist is an oppressive rising-above that presses down on the psalmist's soul, producing prolonged inner sorrow and anguished counsel-taking.
Arrogant speech flows from closed, pitiless hearts — ge.ut names the inner hardness that produces boastful, contemptuous words directed against others.
God sends from on high to rescue — ma.rom here designates the divine height as the source of saving agency directed toward the endangered psalmist, not a human inner characteristic.
Haughty eyes are the visible sign of an inward self-elevation that God actively brings down; rum here describes the proud person's posture that God opposes as a structural opposite to humility.
The Lord is exalted as the living God of salvation — rum here expresses the proper elevation of divine glory, standing as the antithesis of any human arrogation of such honor.
God exalts the psalmist above violent enemies — a relational rising-above granted from outside, countering the inner defeat and vulnerability the psalmist faced.

Presumptuous sins are arrogant inner impulses seeking to gain dominion over the will; the psalmist prays to be kept back from them, recognizing their power to override conscience and produce great transgression.
The congregation calls God to be exalted in His own strength — rum here is the proper upward direction of divine glory, which the worshipping community celebrates with song.
God lifts the psalmist high upon a rock — an exaltation of the endangered self to a place of security, acting on the inner person's fear by raising it above the threat.
The head being lifted up above surrounding enemies transforms inner defeat into confident joy; exaltation here is the restoration of dignity and agency that enables worship.
The psalmist extols God for having drawn him up from danger — rum reflects the inner response of gratitude toward the one who raised the psalmist's life above the reach of his foes.
Pride here is the inner attitude that emboldens lying lips to speak insolently against the righteous — it is the motivating disposition behind contemptuous, oppressive speech.
Acting in pride is a chosen inner orientation that the Lord repays abundantly; it is set against faithfulness, showing pride as a willful posture that incurs divine retribution.
The soul itself is the faculty that boasts — here boasting is an act of the innermost self directed entirely toward the Lord, producing gladness in the humble who hear it.
Exalting God's name is a communal inner act of the will — rum here designates the proper upward direction of praise, lifting the Lord rather than the self.
Arrogance is personified as a foot that tramples and a hand that drives away — an aggressive inner force in the wicked that the psalmist prays will not reach and overwhelm him.
God's exaltation of the one who waits and obeys is a reward directed at the inner posture of trust — rum here is divine elevation that vindicates patient, faithful dependence.
Exaltation to inherit the land is the outcome promised to the one whose inner stance is waiting on the Lord — rum marks the divine reversal that lifts the faithful over the wicked.
The proud are those to whom the blessed person does not turn — ra.hav names an inner disposition of arrogant self-assertion that leads others astray, and which trust in God protects against.
Boasting continually in God is a sustained inner orientation of the whole community — the self finds its ground of confidence not in its own achievement but entirely in the divine.
God declares He will be exalted among the nations — rum here asserts divine supremacy over all earthly powers, the opposite of any human self-elevation that would rival it.
The pride of Jacob is God's chosen heritage — ga.on here carries positive meaning: the dignified excellence of the land God loves and gives as an expression of His favor.
Boasting of the abundance of one's riches is shown as the inner confidence of those who trust in wealth — the self is oriented toward material possession as its source of pride and security.
Boasting of evil is the proud inner posture of a powerful person whose will is directed toward harm; it is set against God's enduring steadfast love as its moral opposite.
Enemies attack the psalmist 'proudly' — ma.rom here describes the inner elevation and contempt with which many assailants press down on the vulnerable, trampling from a position of felt superiority.
The psalmist calls God to be exalted above the heavens — rum here is the desired ascent of divine glory that surpasses all earthly threats, expressing the soul's longing for God's supremacy.
The refrain of divine exaltation is repeated as the psalmist's sustained inner cry — rum expressing that only God's elevation above all else can satisfy the soul in its distress.
Pride is the inner snare in which the enemies are to be trapped by their own words — it is the self-confident arrogance of their cursing and lying lips that becomes the instrument of their ruin.
The rock higher than the psalmist is the object of his faint-hearted cry — rum here points to a transcendent security above the self's own reach, sought from a place of inner weakness.
Those who swear by God shall exult — ha.lal here is joyful inner triumph expressed outwardly, set against the silencing of lying mouths as its moral and relational counterpart.
The upright in heart are called to exult — ha.lal here is inner jubilation arising from a heart aligned with God, an inner integrity that finds its expression in glad, boastful praise.
The rebellious are warned not to exalt themselves — rum here names the inner self-elevation that opposes God's eternal sovereign rule, a pride that God's watchful eyes do not ignore.
God ascends on high in triumphant victory — ma.rom here is the height of divine sovereignty to which God returns after conquest, dwelling among even the rebellious by sovereign grace.
God's majesty over Israel and power in the skies is the divine grandeur to which all power is ascribed — ga.a.vah here names the awesome, sovereign magnificence that belongs to God alone.
Height here describes God's righteousness reaching the loftiest heavens, framing divine moral excellence as transcendent elevation rather than any human inner quality of pride.
Pride is worn like a necklace — an outward display rooted in an inner disposition of self-exaltation — paired with violence, showing that this inner arrogance inevitably manifests in harm to others.

The wicked speak from a position of felt superiority, using lofty threats as a weapon — the inner arrogance expresses itself through the mouth in malice and oppressive speech directed downward at others.
The command not to lift up the horn warns the boastful against self-elevating assertiveness — the inner impulse to exalt oneself is named as belonging to the wicked and is directly prohibited.
Speaking with a haughty neck pictures pride as a bodily posture driven by an inner elevation of self — the prohibition targets the will to position oneself above others through arrogant speech.
The warning not to lift up the horn targets the inner impulse of self-exaltation expressed physically and verbally — the haughty neck makes clear it is an inner attitude manifesting outwardly.
Lifting up does not originate from any earthly direction or human source, implying that self-exaltation pursued independently of God is groundless — the inner drive to rise has no valid human origin.
Exaltation belongs solely to God's sovereign judgment — he raises or humbles whom he will, undercutting any inner assumption that a person can secure their own elevation through pride or striving.
Exaltation is shown to be God's exclusive prerogative in judgment, implying that any inner claim to self-elevation is an usurpation of divine authority over human status.
The wicked who presume to exalt themselves will be cut down, while the righteous are lifted up by God — self-elevation born of arrogance ends in humiliation, while God-given exaltation stands.
Arrogant men are characterized by rising up against others and refusing to set God before them — their insolence is rooted in an inner disregard for divine authority, which drives hostile aggression.
The majesty here is the raging sea's proud surging, which God alone can still — the term names an overwhelming, uncontrollable force that only divine authority can subdue.
The righteous are exalted through God's righteousness, not their own pride — their elevation is a gift flowing from relationship with God, contrasting with arrogant self-elevation.
The horn is exalted by God's favor, not the person's own striving — strength and elevated status are traced entirely to divine bestowal, not inner pride or self-assertion.
God exalts the chosen one from among the people — the elevation is entirely God's initiative, not the result of self-promotion, placing divine agency against any inner arrogance of self-selection.
The horn's exaltation is grounded in God's faithfulness and steadfast love, not the recipient's own merit or pride — status is granted from outside, not seized from within.
God has exalted the foes of his anointed — here exaltation is a divine act of judgment against his servant, showing that lifting up can serve as punishment, not reward for arrogance.
The term pride here refers to what life's best span amounts to — toil and trouble — undercutting any inner boast about human strength or longevity; life's supposed glory yields only fleeting sorrow.
God alone is on high forever — height as a permanent attribute belongs only to the Lord, implicitly contrasting divine transcendence with any transient human claim to elevation or pride.
God has exalted the psalmist's horn — the inner experience of being lifted up in strength and honor is attributed entirely to divine action, not self-assertion or pride.
Majesty is the LORD's royal robe — it describes God's transcendent sovereign dignity, framing divine grandeur as the only legitimate majesty, against which any human pride is implicitly diminished.

The Lord on high is mightier than the most overwhelming natural forces — his height denotes absolute transcendent power, contrasting divine supremacy with any merely creaturely greatness or arrogance.

The proud are those who deserve divine retribution — their inner arrogance is an offense against God's judicial authority, and they are called to account specifically because of that elevated self-regard.
Boasting in worthless idols is exposed as shameful — the inner act of making idols one's glory is shown to be misplaced pride directed at objects that carry no real worth or power.
The LORD is exalted over all peoples — his elevation is universal and absolute, framing all human claims to supremacy as subordinate to divine lordship.
The call to exalt the LORD summons the worshiper's will to actively honor God's supreme status — the inner disposition of reverence is engaged in lifting God above all.

Exalting the LORD at his holy mountain involves both inner devotion and communal worship — the action of lifting God high is paired with holiness as its ground, directing all elevation away from self toward God.

A haughty look paired with an arrogant heart locates pride simultaneously in outward expression and inner disposition — the heart is named as the constitutional seat of arrogance, and both together are intolerable.
Enemies use the psalmist's name as a curse in their deriding taunts — the raving quality of their mockery suggests a frenzied, contemptuous inner attitude that overflows into daily verbal abuse.
God's holy height is the vantage point from which he looks down on the earth — height here marks divine transcendence as the source of attentive compassion, not prideful indifference.

Glorying in God's holy name is the proper direction of boasting — the heart of those who seek the LORD is called to rejoice and boast, redirecting the inner impulse of pride toward God rather than self.
The psalmist desires to glory with God's inheritance — boasting here is a communal act of joy directed outward toward God and his people, the inner faculty of pride properly channeled into worship.
The congregation is called to extol God publicly — the act of lifting God high in praise is a communal inner-to-outer movement of honor directed entirely away from human self-exaltation.
The prayer asks God to be exalted above the heavens — the worshiper's will actively desires divine elevation to surpass all things, implicitly subordinating every human claim to greatness.
The king lifts up his head after drinking from the brook — the lifting of the head signals inner resolve and renewed dignity granted through God's provision, not self-generated pride.
The righteous man's horn is exalted in honor as a consequence of generous giving — his elevated status is God's response to humble, outward-focused generosity, the opposite of proud self-seeking.
The LORD is high above all nations — his elevation is absolute and universal, establishing that no nation or person can claim comparable status, deflating any corporate or individual pride.
God raises the poor and needy from the lowest places — his exalting work runs contrary to proud human hierarchies, showing that true elevation comes from divine compassion directed toward the humble.
The psalmist extols God as an act of gratitude — the inner response to divine help is expressed as lifting God's name high, channeling the praise impulse entirely toward God rather than self.

Arrogant ones are rebuked and accursed because they wander from God's commandments — their insolence is defined by an inner refusal of divine authority, making self-will the essence of their arrogance.

The insolent utterly deride the psalmist, yet he remains steadfast in God's law — arrogance manifests as contemptuous mockery aimed at the law-keeper, but fails to displace the inner commitment of the faithful.
The arrogant smear the psalmist with lies — their insolence expresses itself in deliberate deception, a social weapon of pride; the psalmist's response is whole-heart adherence to God's precepts.

The insolent have wronged the psalmist with falsehood — their arrogance acts through unjust deception, and the desired outcome is shame upon them, while the psalmist's inner life turns to meditation on God's word.
Arrogant people dig pitfalls — their insolence is active and predatory, driven by contempt for God's law; their inner disregard for divine order translates into scheming harm against the faithful.
The psalmist pleads for God's pledge so the arrogant will not oppress — the insolent's inner drive is to dominate and crush, and only divine intervention can restrain that predatory pride.

The proud are those at ease whose inner contempt overwhelms the psalmist's soul — pride here is felt by its victim as crushing scorn, showing the relational destructiveness of an arrogant inner disposition.

The heart not lifted up and eyes not raised high describe an inner humility that consciously refuses self-exaltation — pride's absence is located in heart and eyes, marking a deliberate inner posture of lowliness.

God increased the psalmist's strength of soul when he called — the inner being is emboldened not by self-assertion but by divine response to prayer, grounding assertive inner strength in dependence on God.
The haughty are known by God only from afar — their inner arrogance creates distance from God, who draws near to the lowly instead; pride is shown to alienate the person from divine relationship.

Though the LORD is high, he regards the lowly and keeps the haughty at a distance — divine exaltation is paired with condescending grace toward humility, while human haughtiness is met with relational remoteness.
The proud person's inner disposition drives outward hostile action — they secretly lay traps and snares, showing that arrogance produces calculating aggression against others.

Being exalted here is the feared outcome of God granting the wicked their desires — elevation of the self is tied to the fulfillment of evil plots, making exaltation the reward of wickedness if unchecked.
The psalmist directs exaltation upward toward God as King, expressing that the inner impulse to extol and bless belongs rightly to God, not the self.
God raises up a horn for his people, meaning exaltation here flows from divine initiative outward to a community — it is honor bestowed, not self-seized.
Wisdom, when prized and embraced, will exalt the one who values her — elevation of the person comes as a consequence of right inner orientation toward wisdom rather than self-assertion.
Being assertive here is directed outward as urgent, humble pleading with a neighbor to escape a rash commitment — boldness is channeled into self-correction, not self-promotion.
Haughty eyes appear first in a list of things the Lord hates, locating pride in the gaze — an outward expression of an inward elevation of self that God finds abominable.
Pride is listed alongside arrogance and perverted speech as objects of divine hatred — wisdom's voice defines pride as intrinsically evil, opposed to the fear of the Lord.
Arrogance is paired with pride and evil conduct as something wisdom hates — it is framed as an inner disposition incompatible with fearing God and walking rightly.
Arrogance brings disgrace as its direct consequence, contrasted with the humble who gain wisdom — the inner disposition of self-elevation leads to public shame rather than honor.
A city is exalted by the blessing that flows from the upright — elevation here comes through righteous character acting outward for the communal good, not through pride.
Arrogance produces nothing but strife — the inner disposition of insolence generates relational conflict, while those willing to take advice find wisdom instead.
A hasty temper exalts folly — unrestrained anger lifts up foolishness, showing that lack of inner self-control elevates what should be brought low.
Righteousness exalts a nation — collective moral uprightness raises a people, making exaltation the fruit of right character rather than pride or self-assertion.
The proud person's household is torn down by God, contrasted with his protection of the widow — pride leads to divine opposition and destruction of what the proud have built.
Arrogance is located specifically in the heart, making it a deep inner disposition — the Lord considers such heart-pride an abomination that will certainly be punished.
Pride precedes destruction — it is an inner disposition that moves the person toward ruin, paired with a haughty spirit that precedes a fall, showing pride's self-destructive trajectory.
A haughty spirit (height of spirit) precedes a fall — the inner elevation of spirit named here is the immediate precursor to collapse, showing pride's inward seat in the spirit.
The proud are those who divide spoil — pride here is associated with the powerful who gain by conquest, but lowliness of spirit is commended as better despite material poverty.
Boasting here is a self-serving inner duplicity — the buyer feigns dissatisfaction to drive down a price, then boasts privately once he has secured the advantage, revealing cunning self-promotion.
Haughty eyes and a proud heart together are named as sin — height of gaze is paired with the heart, locating pride in both inner disposition and outward expression as morally sinful.
The arrogant man is identified as a scoffer — his inner haughtiness expresses itself in contemptuous speech and action, making arrogance the defining characteristic of one who mocks.
Arrogance is the defining inner quality of the scoffer — the verse shows pride acting itself out with deliberate arrogant behavior, making insolence both disposition and deed.
The height of the heavens serves as a metaphor for the unsearchable depth of a king's heart — height here measures inscrutability of inner thought rather than pride.
Boasting of a gift never given is empty show — the inner self-promotion produces no real benefit, like clouds and wind without rain, exposing boasting as substance-less self-inflation.

Boasting about tomorrow assumes a certainty over the future the boaster does not possess — the inner presumption of self-confidence about what one will do is checked by human ignorance of what each day brings.
Pride brings its bearer low — the inner disposition of self-exaltation reverses into humiliation, while lowliness of spirit produces honor, showing pride's self-defeating inner logic.
Lofty eyes and high-lifted eyelids portrait a type whose inner arrogance manifests in the gaze — the physical bearing reveals an inner sense of superiority directed contemptuously at others.
Laughter is called mad — the raving, disordered quality of unrestrained pleasure-seeking is exposed as irrational and without genuine inner value or purpose.

Oppression drives the wise into madness — external moral corruption (bribery, oppression) destabilizes inner rational clarity, showing that wisdom's inner seat in the mind is vulnerable to moral disorder.
Pride is located in the spirit — the proud in spirit is contrasted with the patient in spirit, framing inner haughtiness as a disposition that makes a person worse, not better, in the end.
The beloved's gaze overwhelms — assertive inner power is attributed to the woman's eyes, which overpower the speaker, showing boldness or intensity as a relational force rather than arrogance.
God's majesty is so terrible that people flee into rocks and dust to hide from it — pride here describes divine splendor, not human arrogance, representing awe-inspiring transcendent glory.
Haughtiness is expressed in the looks of men and will be brought low — the outward gaze reflects an inner disposition of self-elevation that God will forcibly humble on the day of judgment.

The lofty pride of men will be humbled while the Lord alone is exalted — human inner elevation is contrasted with divine exaltation, showing that human height-pride conflicts with God's sole supremacy.
God's day of judgment targets everything proud and lofty — the proud person's inner self-elevation makes them an object of divine opposition, destined to be brought low.
Everything lifted up and lofty will be brought low on the Lord's day — self-exaltation is named as the target of divine judgment, showing that elevation of self stands against God.
The haughtiness of man will be humbled — the inner disposition of self-elevation is named as something that will be forcibly reversed by God, so only the Lord stands exalted.
The lofty pride (height) of men will be brought low — human inner elevation is shown as temporary and subject to divine reversal, with God's exaltation as the only lasting height.
People flee the splendor of God's majesty into caves — divine majesty here is terrifying glory that overwhelms human beings, not human pride, yet contextually contrasts with human self-elevation.
The splendor of divine majesty drives people into hiding — God's glory is the terrifying polar opposite of human pride, showing that true majesty belongs to God and reduces humans to flight.
The youth being insolent to the elder shows assertive self-will expressed as social aggression — inner boldness becomes contemptuous disregard for rightful order and dignity.
The fruit of the land will be the pride and honor of Israel's survivors — pride here carries a positive sense of God-given glory and distinction belonging to a restored remnant people.
The haughty are brought low along with all humanity — inner haughtiness, expressed in the eyes, is specifically named as what is humbled, making elevated self-regard a target of divine judgment.
Pride and arrogance of heart are the inner posture from which Ephraim and Samaria speak defiantly — self-elevation is located in the heart, driving their refusal to acknowledge God's judgment.
The arrogant heart and boastful look of the Assyrian king will be punished — pride is located in both the heart's inner disposition and the eyes' outward expression, and God holds both accountable.
The great in height will be hewn down — physical stature here serves as a figure for proud elevation, and God's terrifying power cuts down whatever is raised high.
The lofty will be brought low by God's power — whatever has exalted itself, whether in stature or pride, is subject to divine cutting down, making self-elevation precarious before God.
God's glorious deeds are the basis for praise — majesty here belongs to God's actions, not human pride, and the inner response called for is song and proclamation to all the earth.
God's warriors are called 'proudly exulting ones' — their pride is directed in service of divine anger, showing exultant self-confidence employed as an instrument of God's judgment.
Pride here is the pompous self-exaltation of the ruthless, displayed outwardly as pomp; God declares he will lay it low as part of his punishment of wickedness.
The arrogant person's inner pride manifests as visible pomp; God announces he will end it, pairing the inner attitude of self-importance with its outward display as objects of divine judgment.
The arrogant person is characterized by presumptuous self-assertion; God singles out their pomp for destruction, treating arrogance as a moral failing that draws divine punishment alongside iniquity.
Babylon's pride is expressed as national splendor and self-glorying magnificence; its coming overthrow like Sodom frames that pride as the very quality that makes its ruin total and exemplary.
The king's inner pride, displayed as pomp and musical grandeur, is brought down to Sheol and replaced with maggots and worms, showing pride's ultimate destination is humiliation and death.

The will declares in the heart an intent to self-exalt to heaven and above God's stars; this inner volitional self-elevation is the essence of the pride portrayed, originating in the heart's ambition.
Moab's pride is a settled inner disposition of self-importance — 'how proud he is' — that drives arrogance, insolence, and idle boasting, none of which is grounded in truth.
Moab's pride is listed alongside arrogance and insolence as a cluster of inner attitudes, all expressed in empty boasting that is explicitly said to be unfounded.
Moab's pride is the foundational inner characteristic reported — 'we have heard of the pride of Moab' — from which his arrogance, insolence, and empty boasting all flow.
Pride here is the pompous self-glorying splendor that God deliberately purposes to defile and dishonor; the inner attitude of self-exaltation becomes the specific target of divine action.
Here the term refers to the Lord's own majesty, not human pride; the redeemed lift their voices in joy over it, so the word denotes divine greatness that elicits worshipful exultation.
The worshipper's inner will actively exalts God in response to his wonderful deeds; the act of exalting is directed outward from the self toward God rather than inward as self-elevation.

Pride is portrayed as an active inner force allied with physical skill and effort; God lays it low together with the hands' craft, showing pride as an attitude that accompanies and corrupts human striving.

The lofty city whose inhabitants dwell at height is humbled to the dust; the term here suggests self-elevated status and position that God dismantles, setting height and humiliation in direct contrast.
The wicked person's inner perceptual faculty is closed to the Lord's majesty even in a land of uprightness; their corruption prevents them from seeing the greatness that should prompt reverence.
The Lord's hand is lifted up as a sign of his power, yet those addressed fail to perceive it; this blindness to divine exaltation will give way to shame when they finally see it.
The proud crown worn by the drunken leaders of Ephraim is their majesty-like splendor; its being a 'fading flower' exposes the inner pretension of self-importance as already decaying.
The proud crown of Ephraim's drunkards will be trampled underfoot; the term's link to self-glorying splendor is here met with the opposite — complete degradation underfoot.

God exalts himself not in self-aggrandizement but in order to show mercy; his self-elevation is directed entirely toward the benefit of those waiting on him, making it the opposite of prideful self-seeking.
The Spirit is poured from on high — height here belongs to God as the source of transforming grace; it describes divine transcendence rather than human self-exaltation.
The Lord's dwelling on high is the source of his filling Zion with justice and righteousness; divine height here is the ground of moral order, the opposite of human pride's lawless self-assertion.
God declares in direct speech his own arising and self-exaltation; the act is volitional and sovereign, a divine assertion of supremacy in contrast to the enemies who have not reckoned with him.
The righteous person dwells in heights as a place of secure refuge provided by God; the height here is a gift of safety rather than a self-assumed elevation born of pride.

Lifting the eyes to the heights is paired with raising the voice in mockery against the Holy One; the gesture of self-aggrandizing defiance directed upward exposes the inner arrogance of the challenger.
Raising the voice is the outward expression of inner arrogance directed against the Holy One of Israel; the act of vocal self-assertion against God is identified as the core offense.
The heights of the mountains are the terrain conquered by boastful chariots; the boast 'I have gone up the heights' reveals an inner attitude of self-congratulatory military pride.
The height of the tallest cedars becomes the object of a boastful campaign of conquest; cutting down lofty trees is the act through which the inner pride of the invader expresses dominance.
Looking upward here is an act of desperate supplication rather than pride; the weary eyes lifted on high belong to a crushed, oppressed spirit seeking divine help, not self-exaltation.

Boasting here is directed wholly toward the Lord — Israel will glory in the Holy One after God scatters their enemies; the inner orientation of boasting is redirected from self to God as its proper object.

All Israel's offspring will glory in the Lord after being justified; boasting is the appropriate inner response of those who receive righteousness from God, with the Lord as sole ground of that boast.

Babylon's love of pleasures is paired with inner declarations of self-sufficiency ('I am, and there is no one besides me'); the voluptuous ease feeds a prideful heart that imagines itself invulnerable to loss.
God's servant is exalted, lifted up, and raised high as a result of acting wisely; the elevation comes from God and is the outcome of faithfulness, contrasting with prideful self-exaltation.

God's height and holiness are paired with his dwelling alongside the contrite and lowly spirit; divine exaltedness is the structural opposite of human pride, and it draws near to those who abandon self-elevation.
God is high and lifted up yet chooses to dwell with the lowly and contrite; the term marks divine transcendence that paradoxically turns toward humility rather than rewarding human self-exaltation.
God promises to make forsaken Zion majestic forever; the term here is a divine gift of exaltation that reverses shame, contrasting with the self-assumed pride of nations that oppressed her.
Glorying in God is the inner act of nations who swear by the living Lord in truth and righteousness; boasting is here the proper orientation of the will — directed toward God rather than self.

The wise, mighty, and rich are each forbidden from boasting in their own attributes; the prohibition identifies self-directed boasting as a misalignment of the inner person that substitutes creaturely assets for God.

The only legitimate boasting is in knowing and understanding the Lord; this reorients boasting from self-reliance to relationship with God, making that knowledge the sole worthy ground of inner exultation.

Judah and Jerusalem's pride is a national inner posture that God will spoil; the deliberate divine act of ruining it frames pride as the characteristic that most directly draws God's corrective judgment.

The prophet's soul weeps in secret over the people's pride; pride is the inner disposition that, by refusing to listen, drives the community toward captivity, and it moves the prophet to anguished grief.
The glorious throne set on high from the beginning is the Lord's sanctuary; height here denotes the eternal, transcendent authority of God's dwelling rather than any human claim to elevated status.
The redeemed sing on the height of Zion and radiate joy; height here is the place of restored, God-given exaltation and communal flourishing, with no element of prideful self-assertion.
The insolent men flatly contradict God's word delivered through Jeremiah, calling it a lie; their arrogance is an inner presumption that trusts their own judgment over divine revelation.

The call to 'rage' addressed to chariots uses the term in its sense of frenzied, uncontrolled fury in battle; it describes the unrestrained, boastful charging energy of warriors rather than reflective inner pride.
Moab is described as 'very proud,' with the inner attitude of pride leading directly to loftiness, arrogance, and haughtiness of heart — the heart is explicitly named as pride's seat.

Moab's arrogance is listed as a distinct facet of his pride cluster; together with loftiness and haughtiness of heart, it shows pride as a multi-layered inner disposition with arrogance as one aggressive expression.
Moab's pride is the reported, publicly known inner characteristic — 'we have heard of the pride of Moab' — that underlies his entire posture of self-importance and haughtiness.

Moab's loftiness is one element of his pride cluster, positioned between 'very proud' and 'his pride and arrogance'; it names the elevated self-regard that accompanies the other manifestations of pride.
The haughtiness named here is explicitly located in Moab's heart, identifying pride's constitutional seat as the inner person; it is the culminating inner attitude in a list of pride's expressions.

Boasting in valleys and treasures is the inner act of a faithless daughter who trusts her resources and imagines herself unassailable; the boast is a self-deceptive inner confidence disconnected from God.
Arrogance is located in the heart and expressed as self-deceptive confidence in physical inaccessibility; the inner conviction of being unreachable blinds Edom to its own vulnerability before God.
Height here is a physical possession — holding the high hill — that the proud heart mistakes for security; it is the outward expression of inner arrogance that God promises to overturn.
Arrogance is the defining inner identity of Babylon ('O proud one'), drawing God's direct opposition and making the arrogant one the specific target of his judgment day.
Arrogance results in a fall with no one to restore the fallen; the proud inner disposition leads to total collapse and devastation, with no relational support remaining.
Height here refers to Babylon's fortified stronghold, used as a symbol of self-secured elevation; even the highest human fortification cannot prevent God's destroyers from reaching her.
Height here is the divine source — 'from on high' — from which God sends judgment; it is not a human characteristic but the exalted position of God acting in sovereign power against Jerusalem.
To exalt here is God's act of elevating the power of Israel's enemies as judicial punishment; the exaltation belongs to the foes, granted deliberately by God who has thrown down his own people.

Arrogance is depicted as something that buds and blooms organically — it has grown to full maturity as the day of doom arrives, suggesting it developed within the people over time and now triggers judgment.
Pride is the inner motive that drove Israel to redirect God's gift of beauty toward self-glorification and idolatry; the beautiful ornament became an instrument of pride rather than worship.
Pride here belongs to the powerful in Israel — it is the inner strength and confidence of the strong — and God directly targets it for termination by bringing the worst of nations against them.
Impetuousness is located in the heart — 'how sick is your heart' — and expressed as brazen, unrestrained harlotry; the inner will is so corrupted it acts without shame or restraint.
Pride is listed as the first and root sin of Sodom, paired with material excess and ease; together these inner and circumstantial conditions produced callousness toward the poor and needy.
Pride is named as the inner condition that prevailed during a period when Jerusalem treated Sodom with contempt; the proud day is the time when the self-elevated one was most dismissive of others.

Height here describes the towering stature of the vine's strong stems among the thick boughs, used as a metaphor for Israel's rulers reaching visible dominance; the height is conspicuous and celebrated.
What is exalted is to be brought low by divine reversal — God overturns established hierarchies; the high inner or social status is no shield against God's sovereign restructuring.
To exalt here is the divine command to lift up the lowly as part of a complete social and political inversion; God initiates the exaltation of the humble as the counterpart to humbling the proud.

Pride here is the emotional and spiritual attachment Israel has to the sanctuary — it is the pride of their power and the delight of their soul — making its profanation the most devastating inner wound.
Egypt's proud might is its inner power and national confidence; God declares it will come down, showing that national pride rooted in military and political strength is subject to divine humiliation.
Egypt's proud might will come to an end when God breaks the yoke bars — the pride is tied to structural power, and its end coincides with the collapse of that power and the exile of her people.
Height is located in the heart — the tree's heart was proud of its own height — showing that outward greatness produced an inner disposition of self-congratulation that precipitated judgment.
Height here is what the great tree's heart was proud of — the soaring stature that reached into the clouds became the object of inner pride, which in turn became the cause of its downfall.

The heart is proud of its height — the inner faculty of the heart is engaged, with self-exaltation rooted in awareness of one's own elevated position; this inner pride is the trigger for divine judgment.

Height here is the ambition to tower above others and set one's top among the clouds; God's judgment is designed to prevent any future tree from aspiring to such elevation, ending the cycle of proud growth.
Egypt's pride is the inner and national confidence that sustains its multitude; God brings ruthless nations to ruin it, so that both the pride and all that depended on it perish together.

Proud might describes Israel's inner strength and national confidence; its end is paired with total desolation — when the proud inner disposition is spent, the land itself becomes empty and impassable.

Pride is described as something one walks in — a habitual inner posture — and God is declared able to humble those who adopt it; Nebuchadnezzar's testimony is that God's power exceeds any proud stance.
Pride is located in the heart being lifted up and the spirit hardening together; these inner movements produced proud behavior that directly caused Belshazzar's deposition and loss of glory.

The heart is the seat of exaltation — military victory lifts the heart — but this inner elevation leads to overreach and failure, showing that pride born of conquest does not guarantee continued success.

The heart shall be exalted after military victory, locating self-exaltation in the inner person; this inner state of inflated confidence is immediately followed by failure, revealing its fatal instability.

The king exalts himself above every god by his own will — the self-exaltation is total, willful, and directed against the divine order; it is an inner posture of supreme self-assertion that speaks blasphemy.

Self-exaltation here is willed and absolute — the king elevates himself above every god through deliberate inner agency; the exaltation is paired with magnifying himself and speaking against God of gods.
Pride testifies against Israel to its own face — it is a self-incriminating inner characteristic that serves as evidence of guilt, leading directly to stumbling and judgment.
Pride testifies against Israel to its face yet Israel does not return to God — the proud inner disposition actively prevents repentance, blocking the relationship between the people and their God.

To raise up is withheld — because the people are bent on turning away, God will not exalt them; the absence of exaltation is the consequence of persistent apostasy, not a comment on inner pride directly.

The heart is lifted up in response to fullness and satisfaction — prosperity produces inner exaltation, and that elevated heart leads directly to forgetting God; self-sufficiency generates inner pride.
God abhors the pride of Jacob — the inner characteristic provokes divine revulsion; it is paired with reliance on strongholds, showing that pride is connected to misplaced trust in human defenses.
God swears by the pride of Jacob — used here ironically as the most solemn thing Israel holds dear; the pride Israel prizes becomes the very ground of God's oath to judge their unremembered deeds.

Arrogance is explicitly located in the heart and described as self-deceptive; the inner conviction of invulnerability — 'who will bring me down?' — is the voice of a pride that has lost contact with reality.

Lofty dwelling expresses the outward form of inner pride — living in inaccessible heights reinforces the proud heart's delusion of security; the physical elevation mirrors and feeds the inner arrogance.

Majesty here belongs to the name of the Lord — it is divine, not human — and the coming shepherd-ruler draws authority from it; this is not human pride but the legitimate greatness of God expressed through his appointed one.
Height here describes God's exalted position — 'God on high' — before whom the worshiper bows; it frames the inner posture of humility required in approaching a God who is elevated above all.
Majesty here is God's gift being restored to Jacob and Israel — it is not self-generated pride but a dignity that was plundered and is now being returned by divine initiative.

Racing madly describes the frenzied, uncontrolled movement of chariots in battle; the term here captures wild, unrestrained momentum rather than inner boasting, depicting the chaos of Nineveh's downfall.

Setting the nest on high is motivated by the desire for safety — height here is a strategy of self-protection born of greed; the inner drive is self-preservation achieved through elevation above harm's reach.

Pride is the inner root cause of Moab and Ammon's taunting and boasting against God's people; the outward speech of arrogance flows from this inner disposition and determines the judgment they receive.
Pride is located among specific people — the proudly exultant ones — who will be removed from the community; their inner exultation produces haughtiness in God's holy space, which he will eliminate.
Pride of Philistia is the inner identity and national confidence that God will cut off; its removal is part of God's judgment that displaces and dismantles Philistia's self-assured existence.
The pride of Assyria will be laid low by God's sovereign act of passing through trouble and striking down opposition; national pride is the last thing to fall when a great power is finally humbled.

The arrogant are wrongly called blessed by disillusioned observers — the inner disposition of arrogance appears rewarded since evildoers prosper and escape; this reflects a crisis of moral perception.

The arrogant are grouped with evildoers and destined for total destruction by fire; their inner disposition of arrogance is not separated from wickedness but defines their category before God's coming judgment.

The term 'be first' here marks the initiating moment of Jesus' public preaching ministry; no inner pride content is evidenced—archō functions as a temporal marker of commencement, not a pride-cluster characteristic.
The term marks the onset of Jesus speaking to crowds about John; it functions as a temporal/narrative marker of initiation with no inner pride or arrogance content evidenced in this verse.
The term marks the point at which Jesus began denouncing unrepentant cities; it is a narrative marker of commencement and does not evidence pride or arrogance as an inner characteristic here.
The term marks the onset of Peter beginning to sink when fear overtook him; it is a temporal marker of a physical/emotional event with no pride-cluster inner-being content evidenced.
The term marks the point at which Jesus began disclosing his coming suffering and death to disciples; it is a narrative marker of initiation with no pride or arrogance content evidenced.

The term marks the onset of Peter rebuking Jesus, an act rooted in presumptuous will and self-confidence that opposes Jesus' declared path—reflecting an inner disposition of presumption, loosely adjacent to arrogance.

The term marks the onset of the wicked servant's abusive behavior toward fellow servants; his inner assumption of unchecked authority produces domineering action, touching the pride-cluster's theme of self-elevated will.
The term marks the onset of the disciples' sorrowful self-questioning at the Last Supper; it is a temporal/emotional marker with no pride-cluster inner content evidenced.
The term marks the onset of Jesus' deep sorrow and distress in Gethsemane; it is a narrative marker of emotional onset with no pride or arrogance inner-being content evidenced.

The term marks the onset of Peter invoking a curse and swearing denial; the act reflects an inner will asserting a false claim under pressure, but the verse focuses on fear-driven denial rather than pride as such.

The term marks the healed man's initiation of freely spreading news despite Jesus' instruction; the eager self-driven proclamation touches the idea of self-willed assertion, though the verse presents no explicit pride content.
The term marks the commencement of Jesus' teaching by the sea; it is a narrative marker of initiation with no inner pride or arrogance content evidenced in this verse.
The term marks the onset of the crowd begging Jesus to leave; it is a temporal marker of communal reaction with no pride-cluster inner-being content evidenced.
The term marks the healed man's initiation of bold public proclamation about Jesus throughout the Decapolis; the confident, self-initiated witness is assertive but grounded in gratitude, not pride.
The term marks the onset of Jesus teaching in the synagogue on the Sabbath; it is a narrative marker of commencement with no inner pride or arrogance content evidenced.
The term marks Jesus' initiation of sending out the Twelve with authority; it is a narrative marker of commissioned action with no pride-cluster inner content evidenced.

The term marks the onset of Jesus teaching the crowd out of compassion; it is a narrative marker driven by compassion, the structural opposite of prideful self-concern, with no pride content evidenced.

Pride (huperēfania) is listed among evils that flow out of a person's heart, placing it constitutionally within the inner person as one of many corrupt inner impulses—here specifically as a heart-sourced vice alongside deceit, envy, and foolishness.

Pride is named as one of the evil things issuing from within a person, identifying it as an inner-heart characteristic alongside wickedness, deceit, envy, and foolishness—located constitutionally in the heart as a source of moral corruption.

The term marks the Pharisees' initiation of arguing with Jesus and demanding a sign; their assertive, testing stance has overtones of self-confident challenge, loosely touching self-assertion, but the verse frames it as testing rather than pride.
The term marks the onset of Jesus teaching about his coming suffering and death; it is a narrative marker of instruction initiation with no inner pride or arrogance content evidenced.

The term marks the onset of Peter rebuking Jesus after his passion prediction; Peter's presumptuous correction of Jesus reflects a self-elevated will that resists submission, touching the pride-cluster's theme of arrogant self-assertion.

The term marks Peter's initiation of self-referential speech highlighting the disciples' sacrifice; the self-assertive claim has a mild boastful tone, but the verse presents it more as appeal than pure pride.
The term marks Jesus' initiation of telling the Twelve what would happen to him; it is a narrative marker of instruction onset with no inner pride or arrogance content evidenced.

The term marks the onset of the ten disciples' indignation at James and John; the anger arises from a rivalry over status and preeminence, touching the pride-cluster's concern with competing for first place and self-elevation.

The term 'be first' here underpins Jesus' reference to rulers who lord it over others and great ones who dominate—depicting the pride-cluster's characteristic of domineering authority as the negative example against which servant leadership is set.

The term marks the onset of Bartimaeus crying out to Jesus for mercy; it is a narrative marker of humble petition with no pride or arrogance inner-being content—indeed the opposite disposition is evidenced.
The term marks the onset of Jesus driving out temple traders; his authoritative initiative reflects righteous zeal rather than pride, and no inner arrogance content is evidenced.
The term marks the onset of Jesus speaking in parables to the crowd; it is a narrative marker of initiated teaching with no inner pride or arrogance content evidenced.
The term marks the onset of Jesus' eschatological discourse warning against deception; it is a temporal marker of instruction initiation with no inner pride or arrogance content evidenced.
The term marks the onset of the disciples' sorrowful self-questioning at the Last Supper; it is a temporal marker of emotional response with no pride-cluster inner-being content evidenced.
The term marks the onset of Jesus' great distress and trouble in Gethsemane; it is a narrative marker of emotional onset reflecting humility and anguish, the structural opposite of pride.

The term marks the onset of guards spitting on and striking Jesus; it is a narrative marker of abusive action reflecting contempt rather than the subject's inner pride, with no pride-cluster content for the actor's inner life.
The term marks the servant girl's renewed accusation against Peter; it is a narrative marker of repeated speech with no inner pride or arrogance content evidenced.

The term marks the onset of Peter invoking curses and swearing denial of Jesus; driven by fear, the act is a self-protective assertion, but no inner pride content is the primary characteristic evidenced here.
The term marks the crowd's initiation of petitioning Pilate for the customary release; it is a narrative marker of communal action with no inner pride or arrogance content evidenced.

The term marks the soldiers' initiation of mocking Jesus as 'King of the Jews'; their contemptuous salute reflects self-elevated disdain directed outward, but the verse focuses on the act of mockery rather than inner pride as a characteristic.

The arrogant are identified by the location of their pride: the thoughts of their hearts—placing arrogance constitutionally within the inner mind/heart—and God's response is to scatter them, showing pride as an inner orientation that incurs divine opposition.

The term marks the warning not to begin saying inwardly 'We have Abraham as our father'—here archō points to the inner initiation of self-assured, heritage-based presumption, touching the pride-cluster's theme of boastful self-reliance in one's privileged identity.
The term marks the commencement of Jesus' ministry at about age thirty; it is a biographical marker of initiation with no inner pride or arrogance content evidenced.
The term marks the onset of Jesus' declaration that Scripture is fulfilled in the hearers' presence; it is a narrative marker of authoritative speech initiation with no inner pride content evidenced.

The term marks the onset of the scribes and Pharisees questioning Jesus' authority to forgive sins; their challenge reflects self-assured religious authority, loosely touching pride-cluster presumption, but the verse focuses on theological dispute rather than inner pride.
The term marks the onset of Jesus speaking to crowds about John after the messengers departed; it is a narrative marker of initiated speech with no inner pride or arrogance content evidenced.
The term marks the onset of the woman's weeping and anointing of Jesus' feet; it is a narrative marker of humble, penitent action—the structural opposite of pride—with no arrogance content evidenced.

The term marks the onset of table guests questioning Jesus' authority to forgive sins; their self-assured theological challenge carries an implicit sense of self-elevated judgment, but the verse focuses on the question of authority rather than inner pride.

The term marks the onset of Jesus denouncing the sign-seeking generation; the crowd's demand for a sign reflects self-presuming expectation, but the verse focuses on Jesus' response rather than evidencing inner pride as a subject characteristic.

The term marks the onset of scribes and Pharisees pressing and provoking Jesus; their aggressive, hostile initiative reflects an assertive, domineering inner stance that seeks to trap and overpower, touching the pride-cluster's domineering characteristic.

The term marks the onset of Jesus addressing disciples about the Pharisees' hypocrisy; it is a narrative marker of initiated teaching with no inner pride content evidenced for the speaker—hypocrisy rather than pride is the named characteristic here.

The term marks the onset of the unfaithful servant beating fellow servants after assuming his master is delayed; his self-elevated assumption of unchecked authority reflects an inner disposition of domineering self-will, directly touching the pride-cluster's theme of arrogant abuse of power.

The term marks the onset of the excluded attempting to gain entry after the door is shut; their expectation of entry despite prior indifference implies presumptuous self-assurance, but the verse's focus is on exclusion rather than evidencing inner pride as a primary characteristic.

The term here marks the start of a desperate plea — the excluded begin to claim prior intimacy with Jesus; it signals the inner shock of sudden realization, not pride or dominance. No clear pride-cluster content in this verse.
The term marks the shameful moment of being displaced to a lower seat; the inner experience is humiliation, the opposite of pride, as presumed status collapses publicly.

The term marks the onset of excuse-making — each invitee begins to prioritize personal interests over the banquet call; the inner posture is self-regarding preference rather than pride-boasting. Minimal pride-cluster content here.

The term marks the point at which onlookers begin to mock a builder who overreached his capacity; the inner content is the contempt directed at presumptuous ambition that fails. Marginal pride-cluster relevance.

The verse records the taunt against a man who began building but could not finish, implying that his undertaking exceeded his means — a cautionary note about presumptuous initiative. Marginal pride-cluster content.

The term marks the prodigal's descent into want after squandering everything; no pride content is active here — the inner state is need and destitution, not arrogance. No pride-cluster content in this verse.
What human society esteems as high or exalted is declared an abomination before God; the inner drive to self-justify and seek honor among peers is exposed as spiritually corrupt at its root.
Boastfulness appears in a vice catalogue as a disposition of the corrupted will — the braggart's inner person is oriented toward self-display and self-aggrandizement in defiance of God and others.
Haughtiness is listed among vices of a mind given over to moral disorder; the inner person is marked by a contemptuous elevation of self above others, paired with insolence and boasting.

Boasting here is directed toward God through the Law — the inner person takes pride in religious identity and divine privilege, making the Law a ground for self-distinction rather than humble obedience.
The one who boasts in the Law while breaking it dishonors God; the inner inconsistency between proud religious claim and actual conduct is exposed as moral self-deception.
Paul argues that the law of faith eliminates all ground for human boasting; pride in one's own moral achievement is shown to be incompatible with justification received entirely by faith.
If Abraham were justified by works he would have a basis for pride before men, but he has none before God; the verse exposes self-congratulatory boasting as void in the divine presence.
Boasting here is redirected outward and upward — the inner person exults not in self but in hope of God's glory, transforming pride's energy into grateful, God-directed joy.
The believer's inner boasting extends even to sufferings, since they are reframed as producing endurance; this is exultation grounded in faith's perspective rather than self-achievement.
The inner boasting is directed wholly toward God through Christ; it is an exultation in received reconciliation, making the object of pride entirely the divine gift rather than any human merit.
Paul warns Gentile believers not to become high-minded about their standing; the inner danger is a proud elevation of self over broken-off Israel, which must be countered by reverent fear.
The command not to think of oneself more highly than warranted addresses the mind directly; haughtiness is located in self-assessment that overreaches the measure of faith God has given each person.
Being haughty is contrasted with associating with the lowly and living in harmony; the proud inner orientation sets the self above others, fracturing the community and opposing humble solidarity.
The term here refers to the Messiah's authoritative rule over the Gentiles — a divine, not prideful, governance. No human pride-cluster inner-being content is evidenced in this verse.
Paul's boasting is localized in Christ Jesus and directed toward his God-given apostolic work; the pride is not self-generated but grounded in what grace has accomplished through him.
God's sovereign choice of the weak and foolish is designed to shut down all human boasting before him; the inner impulse to self-congratulation is denied any foothold by the logic of grace.
All legitimate boasting must be directed to the Lord alone; the inner person's desire for pride in achievement is redirected so that its only proper object is God, not the self.
Boasting in human leaders is forbidden because all things already belong to the believers; the inner attachment to human figures as sources of pride is shown to be unnecessary and divisive.
Being puffed up in favor of one leader against another inflates the inner person with partisan pride; this self-inflation goes beyond Scripture and produces factional arrogance within the community.
Boasting as if one had not received one's gifts exposes the inner illusion of self-origination; the verse directly addresses the will's tendency to claim credit for what was entirely given by God.
Some in Corinth are arrogantly puffed up, presuming Paul will not return; their inner inflation is a contemptuous self-confidence that dismisses apostolic authority.
Paul will expose arrogant persons not by their inflated words but by their actual power; the inner puffed-up state is revealed as empty boasting when tested by real spiritual capacity.
The Corinthians are arrogantly puffed up over a serious moral failure in their community instead of mourning; their inner inflation prevents the grief and corrective action that conscience demands.
The boasting is judged 'not good' because, like leaven, pride spreads corruption through the whole community; the inner self-congratulation blinds the congregation to the moral danger within it.
Knowledge inflates the inner person while love builds up others; the puffed-up self-inflation of intellectual pride is set in direct opposition to love's outward, constructive orientation.
Paul guards his ground for boasting — the fact of preaching without pay — as something he would rather die than surrender; his pride here is in self-denying service, not self-advancement.

Mere compliance with a divine necessity gives no ground for pride; true boasting for Paul could only arise from freely chosen, costly service — showing pride is linked to voluntary agency, not compulsion.
Love is defined partly by the absence of arrogance; the inflated inner self is directly incompatible with love, which does not self-promote or claim superiority over others.
Paul's pride in the Corinthian believers, held in Christ Jesus, is the very thing he stakes as witness to his daily dying; the inner boasting is relational and Christ-framed, not self-serving.
The boast is grounded in conscience's testimony to sincere, grace-enabled conduct; it is the inner person's confident self-assessment of integrity before God rather than self-aggrandizement.
Mutual boasting between Paul and the Corinthians on the day of the Lord frames pride as a future, relational vindication — the inner ground of boasting is shared belonging and faithfulness.

The term marks the inception of self-commendation; Paul questions whether he is beginning again to promote himself, treating self-commendation as a suspect inner impulse needing scrutiny. Pride-cluster relevance is indirect.
Paul contrasts boasting about outward appearance with what is in the heart; genuine boasting is rooted in inward integrity, while the opponents' pride is exposed as superficial and hollow.
The verse distinguishes pride grounded in outward show from that grounded in the heart; true boasting has its basis in inner character, while false pride clings to visible, surface-level credentials.
Paul's great pride in the Corinthians is paired with comfort and overflowing joy amid affliction; the inner boasting is relational confidence in others' faithfulness, not personal self-exaltation.
Paul's boasting about the Corinthians to Titus was proved true; the inner confidence expressed in that boast was validated by the community's actual response, showing boasting grounded in reality.
The boasting before Titus about the Corinthians is confirmed as truthful; pride here is relational commendation of others that is vindicated by their conduct, not self-promotion.

The Macedonians gave beyond their means of their own accord — a self-chosen, internally-motivated generosity; the will acts freely and spontaneously rather than under compulsion or pride. No pride-cluster content evidenced.
Titus went to the Corinthians of his own accord, motivated by his own earnestness; the inner will acts independently and eagerly without external pressure. No pride-cluster content evidenced here.
Paul calls the Corinthians to demonstrate before the churches the love and the boasting about them; the pride is Paul's confident public commendation of the community, needing visible proof.
Paul boasts about the Corinthians' readiness to give to the Macedonians; the inner confidence is in the community's generous character and zeal, which he has held up as a motivating example.

Paul sends brothers to ensure his boasting about the Corinthians' readiness is not shown empty; the pride is reputational and relational, requiring the community's actual preparedness to remain valid.
If Macedonians find the Corinthians unready, Paul's confident boasting would bring humiliation; the inner confidence expressed publicly is vulnerable to being exposed as unfounded, producing shame.

Even if Paul boasts somewhat beyond measure about his apostolic authority, he will not be ashamed, because that authority is God-given and directed toward building up the community rather than self-glorification.
Paul constrains his boasting to the sphere God assigned him, showing that legitimate self-commendation is bounded by divinely given limits rather than self-determined ambition.
Paul refuses to claim credit for others' gospel work; his boasting is restrained by integrity, directed only toward what God has genuinely accomplished through him.
Boasting in another's field means taking credit for work one did not do; Paul's goal is to preach where no one has, so that any boast reflects genuine, original labor.
All legitimate boasting must be directed toward the Lord as its sole object, redirecting the inner impulse to self-glory away from the self and toward God.
Paul's boasting is grounded in Christ's truth residing in him, making it a conscientious, inward conviction that he will not suppress regardless of geography or opposition.
Paul exposes rivals whose boasting falsely claims parity with his ministry; their boast is a self-serving assertion about their mission rather than a truthful account of their work.
Paul ironically asks to be indulged as a fool so he can boast; the framing acknowledges that boasting is ordinarily the posture of the foolish, not the wise.
Paul explicitly labels his boastful confidence as foolish speech, not the Lord's manner, revealing that this kind of self-assertive pride is contrary to the mind of Christ.

Many boast 'according to the flesh,' making external, worldly credentials the basis of self-promotion; Paul mirrors this ironically, exposing flesh-based boasting as the common but misguided standard.
When boasting is unavoidable, Paul inverts its usual direction: instead of showcasing strength or achievement, he boasts of weakness, making vulnerability the content of his self-disclosure.
