Paul presses on with boasting despite knowing it yields no spiritual gain, pointing to visions and revelations as the reluctant content, not personal achievement or status.
Paul distinguishes boasting on behalf of another (the man caught up to paradise) from boasting about himself; his own boasting is strictly limited to his weaknesses.
Paul could boast truthfully of genuine experiences but voluntarily refrains so that others judge him by observable reality, not inflated self-report—showing self-restraint over self-promotion.
Paul gladly boasts of weaknesses because Christ's power is perfected there; boasting is fully redirected from self-sufficiency to dependence, making weakness the very ground of glory.
Conceit (fusiōsis) appears as one item in a cluster of relational sins Paul fears finding in Corinth, locating it as an inner inflation that generates communal disorder and conflict.
Genuine pride in one's work must arise from honest self-examination, not comparison with others; the boast is grounded in personal integrity rather than competitive self-elevation.
The circumcision party's boasting is in converts' bodies, exposing a self-serving desire to use others as trophies for their own pride rather than genuine zeal for the law.
Paul's only boast is the cross, by which the world is dead to him; this utterly displaces self as the object of pride, substituting crucifixion for any fleshly credential.
Salvation by grace through faith deliberately excludes works so that no person has grounds to boast; human self-exaltation before God is ruled out at the structural level of salvation.
Paul's return to the Philippians will give them cause to glory in Christ Jesus, making the object of their pride Christ himself rather than Paul as a person.
On the day of Christ, Paul anticipates a pride grounded in having run faithfully and not in vain; this is a forward-looking, eschatological form of legitimate satisfaction in faithful ministry.
True worship places all boasting in Christ Jesus and none in the flesh; the inner disposition is wholly Spirit-directed, leaving no room for fleshly self-congratulation as religious credential.
Being 'puffed up' arises from a sensuous mind inflated without factual basis, showing that this conceit is rooted in ungrounded mental fantasy rather than genuine spiritual knowledge.

The Thessalonian believers themselves are Paul's boast before Christ at his coming; pride here is relational and eschatological, grounded in genuine fruit of ministry rather than personal achievement.
Paul openly boasts about the Thessalonians in the churches for their endurance and faith under persecution, directing communal pride outward toward the growth of others rather than self.
A new convert who is given leadership too quickly may become internally puffed up with conceit, and that inflated self-regard leads to the same condemnation the devil incurred.
Conceit is paired with a complete lack of understanding; the inflated inner state blinds the person to truth and fuels a craving for controversy that poisons relationships.
The wealthy are warned against an elevated self-regard tied to material prosperity; haughtiness here is the inner disposition of placing trust and identity in riches rather than in God.
The braggart appears in an end-times character list alongside self-love and arrogance, depicting boastfulness as one expression of a thoroughly self-centered inner disposition.

Arrogance is listed as a defining inner trait of last-days people alongside self-love and boasting, showing it as part of a cluster of self-exalting dispositions that displace regard for God and others.
Self-love heads the list of end-times vices, establishing the inward orientation toward the self as the root from which pride, boasting, and arrogance all flow.

Lack of self-control appears among moral failures of the last days; within this pride cluster it represents the will's inability to restrain impulses, contributing to the broader collapse of inner discipline.
Being 'swollen with conceit' is grouped with recklessness and love of pleasure over God, showing an inner inflation that distorts judgment and inverts proper values.
An overseer must not be self-willed (authadēs); this disposition—asserting one's own preferences without regard for others or God—disqualifies from leadership as incompatible with stewardship.
Holding fast to confident boasting in hope is part of what constitutes belonging to God's household; here pride in hope is a persevering inner grip on what has been promised.
The lowly believer is told to boast in his exaltation, inverting normal pride: the one with no worldly status finds legitimate inner joy in the dignity God has granted him.
God actively opposes the proud, while granting grace to the humble; arrogance here is the inner stance that puts the self in opposition to God and forfeits divine favor.
Arrogance (alazoneia) is the inner disposition that underlies the boasting James condemns; the boast is rooted in a presumptuous self-confidence that excludes God from one's plans.
Boasting here arises from arrogance and is flatly called evil; it is the self-assertive speech of people who presume control over the future without reference to God's will.
This boasting is declared evil in its totality; the pride expressed in arrogant speech about future plans is a moral failure because it excludes God's sovereignty from the inner calculus.
The verse uses 'begin' (archō) in a temporal-structural sense about judgment's starting point; no inner-being content for the cluster characteristic of pride or arrogance is evidenced here.
God opposes the proud, setting arrogance in direct relational conflict with God himself; the antidote is clothing oneself in humility, showing pride as an inner disposition God resists.
Self-willed people despise authority and do not tremble before the glorious ones; their inner will is set entirely on its own course, ungoverned by reverence or submission to any external authority.
The 'bold man' is bold in blasphemy and refuses to tremble; his daring is not courage but a reckless inner presumption that overrides proper fear and produces irreverence toward divine beings.

The pride of life (alazoneia) is identified as a worldly, not God-originating, desire; it is an inner orientation of self-display and presumptuous confidence rooted in the world's values rather than the Father.
